rows.v: Apple, Pear, Peach, Plum, Cherry, Fig
Late summer through fall — best eaten ripe, raw, or baked into desserts.
Apples and pears are 'pomes' — the part you eat is the swollen base of the flower, while the true fruit is the papery core around the seeds.
Orange, Lemon, Lime, Grapefruit, Mandarin
Winter — brighten up the darkest months with fresh juice, zest, and salads.
Almost every citrus you buy is a hybrid of just a few wild ancestors — citron, pomelo, and mandarin — endlessly crossed into oranges, lemons, limes, and grapefruit.
Banana, Mango, Pineapple, Papaya, Kiwi, Pomegranate
Available year-round from warm climates — best ripened at room temperature before eating.
Botanically the banana is a berry, and the plant is a giant herb, not a tree — its 'trunk' is just tightly packed leaf bases.
Watermelon, Cantaloupe, Grape, Avocado
Summer for melons and grapes — avocado is available year-round and ripens off the tree.
Avocados refuse to ripen on the tree — they only soften after picking, so growers use the tree as natural storage and harvest on demand.
Spinach, Kale, Swiss Chard, Arugula, Romaine Lettuce, Bok Choy
Cool-season crops — best raw in salads or quickly wilted to preserve nutrients.
A whole pan of raw spinach cooks down to almost nothing — the leaves are mostly water held in a thin structure that collapses the moment it hits heat.
Broccoli, Cauliflower, Brussels Sprouts, Cabbage
Fall and winter — roasting at high heat brings out natural sweetness and reduces bitterness.
Broccoli, cauliflower, cabbage, kale, and Brussels sprouts are all one species — centuries of breeding one wild mustard for different parts: flower, leaf, and bud.
Carrot, Beet, Sweet Potato, Potato, Radish, Onion, Garlic
Fall harvest, but store well year-round — the backbone of soups, roasts, and sofrito.
The potato is a tuber — a swollen underground stem, not a root — which is why its 'eyes' can sprout whole new plants; keep it out of light, since green patches mean bitter solanine.
Bell Pepper, Tomato, Cucumber, Zucchini, Green Peas, Asparagus
Summer — at their peak raw or lightly cooked to preserve their fresh, crisp character.
Tomatoes, cucumbers, peppers, and zucchini are all botanically fruits — they hold the seeds — even though the kitchen treats every one of them as a vegetable.
